g.data-narration: Bitcoin transactions are signed data structures that transfer value from inputs to outputs using the UTXO model.
A Bitcoin transaction is a digitally signed message that authorizes bitcoin transfer from one address to another.
g.text: A digitally signed message authorizing value transfer between addresses.

g.data-narration: Every Bitcoin transaction has Version, Inputs referencing previous UTXOs, Outputs creating new UTXOs, and Locktime.
Each input references a previous UTXO with Previous Transaction Hash, Output Index, ScriptSig, and Sequence Number.
Each output contains Value (satoshis) and ScriptPubKey defining spending conditions like P2PKH or P2SH.
Bitcoin uses the UTXO model - unspent outputs must be entirely consumed, with change returning to sender.
g.text: Bitcoin uses Unspent Transaction Output model, NOT account balances.
Entire UTXOs must be spent - any leftover becomes change address.

g.data-narration: Alice has two UTXOs worth 0.8 BTC total. She spends both to send 0.6 BTC to Bob with 0.01 BTC fee.
Bitcoin supports multiple script types: P2PKH (Pay-to-PubKey-Hash), P2SH, P2WPKH (SegWit), and P2WSH.
P2PKH scripts execute on a stack: signatures and public keys are pushed, then OP operations verify ownership.
g.text: Stack: 1)Push Sig, 2)Push PubKey, 3)OP_DUP dupes it, 4)OP_HASH160 hashes,

g.data-narration: Four main transaction types: Standard P2PKH, Coinbase (creates new BTC), Multisig (M-of-N), and SegWit.
g.text: Coinbase: No previous TX, creates new BTC, matures in 100 blocks

g.data-narration: Transaction lifecycle: Create → Sign → Broadcast → Mempool → Mining → Confirmation (6 needed for finality).
Transaction fees = Sum(Inputs) - Sum(Outputs), calculated in satoshis per byte for fee rate prioritization.
g.text: Higher fee = faster confirmation | Low: hours-days | Medium: 1-3 hrs | High: 10-30 min

g.data-narration: Nodes verify: valid inputs (UTXOs exist), valid signatures, sufficient funds, script validation, and size limits.
Transaction malleability let attackers change TXID before confirmation. SegWit fixes this by separating witness data.
g.text: TXID could change without invalidating (signature in hash)
Separates signature from TX, fixes malleability, enables Lightning
SegWit Benefits: Smaller tx size, lower fees, fixes malleability, enables second-layer solutions

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: UTXO model, transaction components (Version, Inputs, Outputs, Locktime), fee calculation, verification process.
g.text: Exam Tip: Know all components, UTXO model, fee calc, verification steps

g.data-narration: Bitcoin transactions use the UTXO model with inputs referencing previous outputs and creating new unspent outputs.
g.text: Bitcoin Transaction = Inputs (UTXOs to spend) → Outputs (New UTXOs) + Fee to Miner
